questions.prompt: If I am choosing between TCP and UDP, what reliability work does TCP take on that UDP leaves to the application?

answers.text: TCP gives the application one ordered, reliable byte stream instead of a pile of independent packets.

feedback.legit: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: TCP provides an ordered reliable byte stream. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: UDP datagrams may vanish, arrive twice, or show up out of order. The protocol does not repair that for you.

feedback.legit: UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: UDP datagrams can be lost, duplicated, or reordered. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: UDP skips connection setup and lets the application decide whether it needs retries, ordering, or no reliability layer at all.

feedback.legit: UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: UDP avoids connection setup and lets applications choose their own reliability strategy. This one should stay marked Legit.

questions.prompt: When an app asks DNS about a hostname, what kind of answer comes back, and why might a resolver reuse it?

answers.text: DNS also encrypts the application's entire connection automatically. Once the name resolves, TLS is unnecessary.

feedback.legit: DNS resolution and transport encryption are separate mechanisms. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: DNS resolution and transport encryption are separate mechanisms. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: Resolvers can cache a record until its time-to-live expires instead of repeating the lookup every time.

feedback.legit: Resolvers may cache answers until their time-to-live expires. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Resolvers may cache answers until their time-to-live expires. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: DNS maps names to records, including address records that contain IPv4 or IPv6 addresses.

feedback.legit: Names such as hostnames into records including IP addresses. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Names such as hostnames into records including IP addresses. This was accurate as written.

questions.prompt: People call an HTTP method idempotent when retries are safer. What stays the same after repeating the request?

answers.text: Every retry must return the exact same response bytes, headers, timestamp, and status code.

feedback.legit: Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Idempotency concerns the intended server effect; response content can change between requests. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: The intended effect on server state should match the effect of sending the request once.

feedback.legit: Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Repeating the same request has the same intended effect as sending it once. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: Idempotent means side-effect free. Any request that changes stored data is automatically non-idempotent.

feedback.legit: An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: An idempotent request can change state; repetition should not add further intended change. Your vote matches the correction.

questions.prompt: A browser accepts a server's TLS certificate. What identity and key binding has it actually verified?

answers.text: It verifies that a trusted issuer bound the presented public key to the identity the client requested.

feedback.legit: That a trusted issuer bound a public key to the requested identity. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: That a trusted issuer bound a public key to the requested identity. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: That authenticates identity and key material, not whether the server's application code is secure or correct.

feedback.legit: Certificates authenticate identity and keys, not application correctness. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: Certificates authenticate identity and keys, not application correctness. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: Normal validation also checks details such as the requested hostname and the certificate's validity period.

feedback.legit: The hostname and validity period are part of normal verification. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: The hostname and validity period are part of normal verification. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

questions.prompt: A packet crosses a NAT boundary. Which parts of its addressing are commonly rewritten, and what security does that not imply?

answers.text: NAT encrypts the packet end to end. Address translation and confidentiality are the same service.

feedback.legit: NAT changes addressing; encryption requires a protocol such as TLS or IPsec. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: NAT changes addressing; encryption requires a protocol such as TLS or IPsec. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: It is a complete firewall by itself, so separate allow and deny policies only duplicate what NAT already guarantees.

feedback.legit: Address translation is not a substitute for explicit firewall and access-control rules. The original claim needed a Sus vote.
feedback.sus: Address translation is not a substitute for explicit firewall and access-control rules. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: NAT commonly rewrites IP addresses and often ports as traffic moves between network realms.

feedback.legit: Rewrite address and often port information between network realms. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Rewrite address and often port information between network realms. The original wording holds up.

questions.prompt: What does the `/24` part of an IP network tell a host when it decides whether a destination is on-link?

answers.text: The host compares the destination against its configured on-link prefixes before choosing a direct path or a router.

feedback.legit: A host can compare the destination with its on-link prefixes when making a routing decision. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: A host can compare the destination with its on-link prefixes when making a routing decision. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: The prefix length says how many leading address bits identify the network portion.

feedback.legit: Which leading address bits identify the network prefix. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Which leading address bits identify the network prefix. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: It sets the highest TCP or UDP port number available to hosts inside the subnet.

feedback.legit: Ports belong to the transport layer and are unrelated to the subnet prefix. That makes the original claim unsafe to label Legit.
feedback.sus: Ports belong to the transport layer and are unrelated to the subnet prefix. Your Sus vote caught the problem.

questions.prompt: My IPv4 host knows a neighbor's IP address but needs to send an Ethernet frame. What does ARP discover?

answers.text: ARP resolves the neighbor's IPv4 address to its link-layer address on the local network.

feedback.legit: Map an IPv4 address to a link-layer address. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Map an IPv4 address to a link-layer address. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: It resolves public domain names across the internet, acting as a lower-level replacement for DNS.

feedback.legit: DNS resolves names; ARP operates on a local link. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: DNS resolves names; ARP operates on a local link. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: The request is normally broadcast on the local segment so the owner of that IPv4 address can reply.

feedback.legit: ARP requests are normally broadcast on the local network segment. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: ARP requests are normally broadcast on the local network segment. This one should stay marked Legit.

questions.prompt: TCP has both flow control and congestion control. Which one protects the receiver, and which one reacts to the network?

answers.text: Congestion control responds to contention and limited capacity along the network path.

feedback.legit: Congestion control reacts to capacity and contention in the network. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: Congestion control reacts to capacity and contention in the network. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: They are two labels for the same receiver-buffer value. Neither one considers conditions in the network.

feedback.legit: They address different bottlenecks even though both can limit sending rate. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: They address different bottlenecks even though both can limit sending rate. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Flow control keeps a fast sender from overwhelming the receiving endpoint's available capacity.

feedback.legit: Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Flow control protects the receiver from being overwhelmed. This one was safe to mark Legit.

questions.prompt: A browser blocks frontend code from reading a cross-origin response. What is CORS enforcing, and what is it not stopping?

answers.text: CORS stops cross-site request forgery by preventing the browser from sending the request to the server at all.

feedback.legit: CORS mainly controls browser access to responses; CSRF needs its own defenses. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: CORS mainly controls browser access to responses; CSRF needs its own defenses. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: It controls whether browser-based frontend code may read certain responses from another origin.

feedback.legit: Whether frontend code may read certain cross-origin responses. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Whether frontend code may read certain cross-origin responses. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: It is a network firewall for every client. Curl and backend services cannot send packets when CORS denies an origin.

feedback.legit: CORS is a browser security policy, not a network firewall. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: CORS is a browser security policy, not a network firewall. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: QUIC wants reliable streams, so why build it on UDP instead of putting everything back inside TCP?

answers.text: UDP provides datagram traversal while QUIC implements transport behavior in user space, where it can evolve more quickly.

feedback.legit: It can implement transport features in user space while using UDP datagrams for network traversal. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: It can implement transport features in user space while using UDP datagrams for network traversal. The Sus label does not fit this claim.

answers.text: It abandons reliability and ordered streams completely. QUIC is fast only because lost data is ignored.

feedback.legit: QUIC implements reliability and multiple streams above UDP. The original wording does not hold up.
feedback.sus: QUIC implements reliability and multiple streams above UDP. You flagged exactly what was wrong.

answers.text: Separate QUIC streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams when one stream loses data.

feedback.legit: Independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Independent streams reduce transport-level head-of-line blocking between streams. The reply belongs under Legit.

questions.prompt: After the HTTP upgrade to WebSocket succeeds, what communication model replaces ordinary request and response?

answers.text: Every WebSocket message starts a brand-new HTTP request, complete with fresh headers and a separate connection.

feedback.legit: WebSocket uses its own framing after the handshake. That distinction makes the original answer Sus.
feedback.sus: WebSocket uses its own framing after the handshake. Your judgment caught the technical error.

answers.text: The long-lived connection still fails sometimes, so applications need detection, reconnection, and state-recovery logic.

feedback.legit: The long-lived connection still needs failure detection and reconnection logic. Your call matches the underlying rule.
feedback.sus: The long-lived connection still needs failure detection and reconnection logic. This one was safe to mark Legit.

answers.text: Client and server can send framed messages in both directions over the upgraded connection.

feedback.legit: Client and server can exchange framed messages in both directions. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: Client and server can exchange framed messages in both directions. This was accurate as written.

questions.prompt: A large packet fits the first link but not a smaller link later in the route. What is path MTU discovery trying to learn?

answers.text: It is an IPv4-only concern. IPv6 paths never contain links with different MTUs.

feedback.legit: IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: IPv6 also relies on path MTU discovery because routers do not fragment packets in transit. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: Just use the largest MTU found anywhere on the path; every smaller link will adapt automatically.

feedback.legit: The path MTU is constrained by the smallest supported MTU along the route. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: The path MTU is constrained by the smallest supported MTU along the route. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: It chooses the fastest DNS resolver, which then advertises a safe packet size for the route.

feedback.legit: Path MTU concerns packet size, not name resolution. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Path MTU concerns packet size, not name resolution. Good read: the original claim was flawed.
